summary: DHH describes his approach to reducing phone usage by removing all apps from his iPhone's Home Screen after a week-long phone sabbatical inspired by Cal Newport's Digital Minimalism. His Home Screen now shows only three essential apps against a cosmos wallpaper, requiring him to search for any other app. He explains how this adds helpful friction to frivolous usage while keeping intentional use easy, and uses the Photos app as a healthy fallback when the urge to scroll strikes. He acknowledges this isn't a complete solution, noting that replacing phone time with better activities is equally important.
key_points: Removing apps from the Home Screen adds friction that deters frivolous use while keeping intentional use easy
A nearly empty Home Screen prevents the muscle-memory tour of apps that happens when you open the phone for one task
The Photos app serves as 'digital methadone' — a finite, personal alternative to infinite scrolling feeds
Phone sabbaticals alone don't work; lasting change requires replacing phone time with better alternatives
money_quotes: Any use that's actually worth doing is easily worth the friction, but any frivolous use now faces a helpful hurdle to deter it.
I no longer open my phone to do one thing, then get dragged into a muscle-memory tour of other apps while I'm there.
It's far easier to simple do the thing I desired to do, then put the phone away, when I'm literally staring into the cosmos after completing any single task.
The photos app is there as a form of digital metadone.
The only slot machine available on the first row is my personal photo album.
There's no forever scrolling feed of novelty that can suck me in for hours on end. Just a small slice of happy memories.
Escaping the phone addiction takes more than just a quick or clever hack. You have to replace the time spent using the phone with better alternatives.
key_insight: Adding friction to phone usage by clearing the Home Screen transforms the phone from an attention trap back into an intentional tool.
